amber.description: Catalysts, regulatory proteins, molecular machines, receptors

black.description: Terminal products, cellular outcomes, equilibrium states, system endpoints

darkSkyBlue.description: Biochemical reactions, transport, modifications, transcription, translation

green.description: External signals, nutrient availability, environmental conditions, initial states

lightCyan.description: Metabolites, molecular complexes, cellular states, compounds

purple.description: Multi-component requirements, signal integration, convergence points

yellow.description: Decision points with alternative pathways (yes/no branching)

description: The HOG (High Osmolarity Glycerol) pathway in S. cerevisiae is a MAPK signaling cascade that responds to osmotic stress by activating transcription of osmoprotective genes and stimulating glycerol production. The pathway has two input branches: the SLN1 branch (two-component phosphorelay via Sln1-Ypd1-Ssk1 under normal osmolarity) and the SHO1 branch (membrane sensor Sho1-Ste20-Ste50-Ste11). Both branches converge on redundant MAPKKKs (Ssk2/Ssk22) that activate the MAPKK Pbs2, which phosphorylates and activates the MAPK Hog1. Activated Hog1 translocates to the nucleus, phosphorylates transcription factors (Hot1, Msn2/4, Sko1), and induces genes for glycerol synthesis (GPD1, GPP2), ion transporters, and stress proteins. Hog1 also regulates translation, cell cycle, and chromatin remodeling. The pathway exemplifies eukaryotic MAPK architecture with scaffold proteins, feedback regulation, and multi-level signal integration. This flowchart illustrates both input branches, the MAPK cascade, nuclear outputs, and adaptation mechanisms.

name: HOG Pathway (High Osmolarity Glycerol Response)
notes: The HOG pathway is the paradigm for MAPK signaling in eukaryotes, demonstrating key principles: dual input branches for robustness, scaffold proteins (Pbs2) for specificity, dual phosphorylation for MAPK activation, and multi-level outputs (transcription, translation, cell cycle, chromatin). The SLN1 branch uses a two-component system (bacterial-like) while SHO1 uses membrane sensors (eukaryotic). Pbs2 scaffold prevents cross-talk with other MAPK pathways (mating, cell wall). Glycerol synthesis is the major output, increasing internal osmolarity to match external. The pathway is highly conserved: mammalian p38 MAPK is the Hog1 ortholog. HOG has been extensively modeled computationally, making it ideal for systems biology. This pathway exemplifies eukaryotic signal integration with high AND gate requirements for complex assembly.
scientificAccuracy: This flowchart is based on extensive genetic and biochemical studies of the HOG pathway, one of the best-characterized MAPK pathways in eukaryotes. All components (Sln1, Sho1, Ssk2, Pbs2, Hog1, transcription factors), regulatory mechanisms, and signal flow have been validated through decades of research. The model represents the paradigm for osmotic stress signaling.

sources.relevance: Glycerol synthesis as major osmoprotective response
